question: Explain the differences between NoSQL and RDBMS databases.
expectedPoints: NoSQL uses flexible schema while RDBMS uses fixed schema
NoSQL provides horizontal scalability, RDBMS provides vertical scaling
NoSQL documents store related data together (denormalized), RDBMS uses normalized tables with joins
NoSQL handles unstructured data better; RDBMS is better for complex transactions
NoSQL typically lacks ACID properties; RDBMS guarantees ACID transactions

question: What are the advantages of using MongoDB?
expectedPoints: Flexible schema - documents can have different fields
Horizontal scalability through sharding
High performance with embedded documents and indexing
Rich query language supporting CRUD operations
Support for replication and high availability
JSON-like syntax makes it developer-friendly
Automatic failover with replica sets

question: Explain the concept of replication in MongoDB and its benefits.
expectedPoints: Replication creates multiple copies of data across servers
Replica set consists of one primary and multiple secondary nodes
Provides data redundancy and fault tolerance
Enables high availability - if primary fails, secondary becomes primary
Supports read scaling - queries can be distributed to secondaries
Automatic failover ensures uninterrupted service

question: Describe the different data modeling approaches in MongoDB.
expectedPoints: Embedded documents - storing related data together in one document
Referenced documents - using references/links between documents like RDBMS joins
Normalized data - separating data into different collections with references
Choice depends on data relationships, query patterns, and frequency of updates
Embedded is better for one-to-few relationships and read-heavy operations
Referenced is better for many-to-many and frequently updated data

question: What is the aggregation framework in MongoDB? Explain any two pipeline stages.
expectedPoints: Aggregation framework processes data through multiple stages
Pipeline stages transform documents sequentially
$match - filters documents similar to WHERE clause
$group - groups documents by a key and performs aggregations
$project - reshapes documents, selecting/adding/removing fields
$sort - orders documents by specified fields
$limit and $skip - pagination support

question: Explain sharding in MongoDB and why it is used.
expectedPoints: Sharding distributes data across multiple machines
Enables horizontal scaling to handle large datasets
Uses shard key to determine data distribution
Chunks are split when they exceed maximum size
Provides high throughput for large-scale applications
Reduces load on individual servers
Configurable based on application needs

question: Write and explain MongoDB commands for: (a) Creating database (b) Creating collection (c) Inserting a document (d) Querying documents
expectedPoints: USE databaseName - creates/switches to database
db.createCollection('collectionName') - creates collection
db.collection.insertOne({document}) - inserts single document
db.collection.insertMany([{doc1}, {doc2}]) - inserts multiple
db.collection.find() - retrieves all documents
db.collection.find({field: value}) - queries with filter
db.collection.findOne() - retrieves single document

question: Explain the CAP theorem and discuss how MongoDB handles consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.
expectedPoints: CAP theorem states distributed database can provide only 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
Consistency: All nodes see same data at same time
Availability: Every request gets a response (no errors)
Partition tolerance: System works despite network failures between nodes
MongoDB is a CP system - prioritizes consistency and partition tolerance
During network partition, MongoDB becomes unavailable to ensure consistency
Replica set elections ensure consistent primary node
Write operations wait for acknowledgment from majority of replicas
Trade-off: MongoDB sacrifices availability during partitions for consistency

question: Design a MongoDB database schema for an e-commerce application. Consider collections for products, customers, and orders. Explain your design decisions including embedded vs referenced documents.
expectedPoints: Products collection: _id, name, description, price, category, inventory, ratings
Customers collection: _id, name, email, address (embedded), phone (array), createdAt
Orders collection: _id, customerId (reference), products (array with quantity), totalAmount, status, orderDate
Embedded: address in customer, products array in order (one-to-few)
Referenced: customerId in orders (many-to-many or one-to-many)
Denormalization for read performance in orders
Index on email (unique), customerId, orderDate
Benefits: Fast reads, reduced joins, scalable design
Consider: Query patterns, update frequency, data size

question: Explain the CRUD operations in MongoDB with syntax examples for insert, update, delete, and query operations.
expectedPoints: Create: insertOne({doc}), insertMany([{docs}])
Read: find(query, projection), findOne(query)
Update: updateOne(filter, update, options), updateMany(filter, update), $set for updating fields, $inc for increment
Delete: deleteOne(filter), deleteMany(filter)
Query operators: $eq, $gt, $lt, $in, $and, $or, $not
Update operators: $set, $unset, $push, $pull, $addToSet
Options: upsert (insert if not exists), multi (update many)
Example queries demonstrating practical usage

question: Discuss indexing in MongoDB. Explain types of indexes, creation syntax, and how indexes improve query performance.
expectedPoints: Indexes are data structures that improve query speed
Default _id index is automatically created
Single-field indexes: on one field
Compound indexes: on multiple fields (order matters)
Unique indexes: no duplicate values
Text indexes: for text search
Syntax: db.collection.createIndex({field: 1/-1})
1 for ascending, -1 for descending
Indexes consume storage space and slow down writes
Use explain() to analyze query performance
Covering indexes can satisfy queries without examining documents
Index selection based on query patterns
